skill.name: writing-bots
skill.description: Guides assistants through designing, writing, testing, and running Silverback SDK bots for public blockchain event automation.
skill.summary: Create Silverback bots that react to blockchain events with measured operational safeguards.
skill.author: ApeWorX

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified network access, environment-variable guidance, and external command examples, but the weak cryptography and Ruby backtick alerts are false positives from Markdown prose. The skill has legitimate blockchain automation intent, but it can lead users to install a CLI, run bots, use API keys, and broadcast transactions, so publication should include a clear operational risk warning.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Installation and Bot Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs assistants to install Silverback with uv and run a bot using the Silverback CLI. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can execute local project code and connect to blockchain networks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command guidance is explicit and directly tied to bot execution. The surrounding text frames it as normal Silverback usage, not hidden execution.

medium_findings.title: Blockchain Transaction and External Service Automation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill describes bots that may send POST requests, use communication services, and sign or broadcast blockchain transactions. These actions are domain-appropriate but high-impact if generated code is not reviewed and tested.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text clearly names external service calls and transaction broadcasting. It also includes risk controls, which supports legitimate intent but does not remove operational risk.

low_findings.title: Documentation Network Fetch Is Legitimate
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill tells assistants to fetch current Silverback documentation from ApeWorX URLs. This is a network dependency, but it targets public vendor documentation and supports accurate code generation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible, public documentation links. I found no evidence that the network request transmits secrets or user data.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access for Runtime Configuration
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill suggests operational modes and limits based on environment variables such as API keys. This is common configuration practice, but generated bots must avoid logging or transmitting secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment-variable reference is explicit, but it is framed as configuration and risk management. No evidence shows secret exfiltration.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static weak-cryptography findings point to prose, URLs, and metadata, not cryptographic algorithms or insecure crypto APIs. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or related weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language descriptions and documentation references. There is no cryptographic code on those lines.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Execution Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most Ruby backtick execution alerts are Markdown inline code or fenced code formatting, not Ruby code. The actual shell commands are documented separately as an operational medium-risk finding.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the backticks delimit tool names, Python identifiers, and command examples. There is no Ruby execution context in the skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Documentation Fetch
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs assistants to use web_fetch against public ApeWorX documentation before writing code. This is transparent and legitimate, but it is still network access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network destination is explicitly shown and limited to documentation pages. No hidden or encoded endpoint is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Line Installation and Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes command examples for installing Silverback and running a bot on a selected blockchain network. Users should inspect generated code and run against test networks first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear and relevant to the skill. They are not obfuscated, but they can execute code in the user environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret-Adjacent Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill mentions API keys and environment variables for runtime modes and limits. This can be safe, but generated bots need secret-handling safeguards.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text references API keys and os.environ directly. It does not instruct exfiltration or unsafe logging.

content.value_statement: Blockchain automation is risky when bot requirements are unclear or safeguards are missing. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Silverback bot design, implementation, and staged testing.
actual_capabilities: Guides requirement gathering for blockchain networks, contracts, event types, and avoided scenarios.
Structures Silverback bot handlers for new blocks, event logs, cron jobs, and metric-based triggers.
Encourages current documentation lookup before generating Silverback SDK code.
Adds risk controls such as staged testing, environment-based modes, limits, and circuit breakers.
Explains how to install Silverback and run a bot with the target ecosystem and network.
limitations: Requires uv and the Silverback tooling to run generated bots locally.
Depends on current ApeWorX documentation for accurate Silverback APIs.
Does not verify smart contract safety, signer permissions, or economic risk by itself.
Needs user-provided networks, contract addresses, event details, and operational constraints.

use_cases.title: Monitor Contract Events
use_cases.description: Create a Silverback bot that reacts when a chosen smart contract emits specific events.

use_cases.title: Prototype On-Chain Automation
use_cases.description: Draft a bot workflow that starts with logging and later adds controlled transaction signing.

use_cases.title: Design Operational Safeguards
use_cases.description: Plan metrics, limits, stop conditions, and rollout steps before running a blockchain bot.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Event Bot
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a first Silverback bot.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Silverback bot for this network and contract. Ask for any missing event details before writing code.

prompt_templates.title: Add Metrics and Logging
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Silverback bot plan that tracks useful metrics, logs actions clearly, and avoids unnecessary internal state.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Test Rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this bot idea and propose staged testing steps, dry-run behavior, limits, and emergency stop conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Production Automation Spec
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced bot design with external services and signing risk.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Silverback bot that monitors these events, calls these services, and only signs transactions under explicit safety constraints.

output_examples.input: I need a bot that watches transfer events and alerts my team.
output: A requirements checklist for network, contract, event, and alert destination.
A Silverback handler plan for the selected event log.
A testing path that starts with logs before enabling external messages.

output_examples.input: I want a bot that can submit transactions when a metric crosses a threshold.
output: A metric definition tied to on-chain state or recent block activity.
Suggested limits, signer checks, and circuit breaker conditions.
A staged rollout plan using dry-run mode before live transaction submission.

best_practices: Fetch current Silverback documentation before writing or updating bot code.
Start with logging and dry-run behavior before adding signers or external service actions.
Define limits, metrics, and emergency stop conditions before production deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not run a generated bot against mainnet before reviewing and testing it.
Do not expose API keys, signer keys, or secrets in logs or prompts.
Do not add broad transaction permissions without explicit safety constraints.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Silverback SDK bots that listen to blockchain events and react with defined actions.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs assistants to fetch current ApeWorX Silverback documentation before writing code.

faq.question: Can it create bots that send transactions?
faq.answer: Yes, but transaction signing should be added only after review, dry-run testing, and strict limits.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the network, contract addresses, events, desired actions, constraints, and scenarios the bot must avoid.

faq.question: Is this safe for production use?
faq.answer: Production use requires code review, test network runs, secret management, monitoring, and emergency stop controls.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
